digest.top_line: RASolute 302: mOS 13.2 vs 6.7mo (HR 0.40), daraxonrasib roughly doubles OS in previously-treated metastatic pancreatic ca.
digest.tldr: GI posted the day's biggest number (RASolute 302 roughly doubling 2L pancreatic OS), but the RT slate is the reader's story: in prostate, adding enza (ENZARAD) or protons (COMPPARE) to the RT+ADT backbone failed while intraprostatic CAN-2409 improved DFS (HR 0.70); thoracic OCEANUS found sequential iRT beat concurrent (HR 0.68, real-world).

sites.intro: RT-intensification theme dominates: adding enza (ENZARAD) or protons (COMPPARE) to the RT+ADT backbone failed, while intraprostatic CAN-2409 hit its DFS endpoint.

studies.name: COMPPARE
studies.tldr: Proton vs IMRT: no difference in bowel urgency (6% vs 5.7%), ≥G2 GI tox, or 3-yr biochemical control in localized prostate.
details: 🔍 Prospective comparative effectiveness (COMPPARE, PCORI-funded); non-randomized proton vs photon cohorts, 51 centers, 2524 accrued 2018-2022
🔍 De novo localized prostate, excl very-high-risk + metastatic; proton n=1500, photon n=1000
📊 No powered endpoint favored protons; observed rates near-identical, all p≥0.28
🔍 Observed GI tox far below design assumptions (hypothesized IMRT 29%, actual 5.6%), reflecting modern technique + spacers
⚠️ Non-randomized cohorts: selection bias and residual confounding limit the proton-vs-IMRT comparison
⚠️ Early results, short f/u; late GU/GI toxicity (protons' theoretical edge) and long-term control unassessed
🔗 Directionally consistent with PARTIQoL (randomized proton vs IMRT, localized prostate): no bowel-QoL advantage for protons

verdict.rationale: Prospective but non-randomized cohorts (selection bias); early results, short f/u leave late toxicity and long-term control open. Null aligns with randomized PARTIQoL non-superiority.
verdict.audience: De novo localized prostate cancer, excl very-high-risk and metastatic

studies.significance: The RT read is that rectal spacer use, not proton vs photon, moved GI toxicity: 2-yr G2+ fell to ~4.4-4.7% with a spacer vs 7.2-8.7% without (p=0.009), similar across modalities. Protons showed no toxicity or 3-yr biochemical-control edge, arguing against the proton premium for localized disease.
studies.monday_clinic: In localized prostate cancer (de novo, excluding very-high-risk and metastatic), these early data question routine proton use over IMRT for GI toxicity or 3-yr control; they do not yet speak to late toxicity or long-term outcomes.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Prospective comparative effectiveness study (**COMPPARE**, PCORI-funded), **non-randomized** proton vs photon cohorts across 51 centers. 2524 accrued July 2018-October 2022. Early results, short follow-up.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: De novo **localized prostate cancer**, excluding very-high-risk and metastatic. Proton cohort n=1500, photon (IMRT) cohort n=1000.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: Proton therapy vs IMRT; rectal spacer use captured as a covariate (FDA-approved 2015). **Dose/fractionation and target volume not reported in source.**

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: Co-primary patient-reported bowel urgency and frequency (EPIC) and ≥G2 GI toxicity (CTCAE v5), each powered 90%. Exploratory: 3-yr freedom from PSA progression.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: **No significant proton advantage** on any endpoint (all p ≥ 0.28). Observed toxicity fell far below design assumptions (hypothesized IMRT GI tox 29%, actual 5.6%).

analysis_sections.label: Safety
analysis_sections.body: Rectal spacer reduced 2-yr cumulative G2+ GI toxicity in **both arms** (p=0.009); the spacer effect exceeded any proton-vs-IMRT difference.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: Directionally consistent with **PARTIQoL** (randomized proton vs IMRT, localized prostate), which found no bowel-QoL advantage for protons.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents de novo localized prostate cancer treated with definitive protons or IMRT. Does not represent very-high-risk or metastatic disease.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Non-randomized cohorts (**selection bias**, residual confounding); short follow-up leaves late GU/GI toxicity and long-term control unanswered; dose/fractionation not reported.

open_questions: Long-term biochemical and metastasis-free control with protons vs IMRT

related_trials.brief_title: Proton Therapy vs. IMRT for Low or Intermediate Risk Prostate Cancer
related_trials.brief_summary: We are studying whether men being treated for prostate cancer have the same amount of side effects from either one of two different external radiation treatments: IMRT or PBT. With IMRT, a number of x-ray beams are used to shape the radiation to the prostate. PBT is another type of external radiation treatment for prostate cancer that is used in a few centers in the United States. Protons are tiny particles with positive charge that can be controlled to travel a certain distance and stop. PBT…
related_trials.eligibility_brief:
Inclusion Criteria:

* Diagnosed with histologically confirmed adenocarcinoma of the prostate based on core-biopsy within 1 year of study entry from TRUS
* Clinical stages T1c to T2c
* PSA \<20, within 6 months of study entry
* Participants who are currently receiving Dutasteride (or have received…

* Localized prostate cancer, as confirmed by staging with PSA, biopsy, Gleason score, DRE with or without mpMRI, and clinical…

related_trials.brief_title: Mild Hypofractionation With Proton Therapy or IMRT for Intermediate-Risk Prostate Cancer
related_trials.brief_summary: This is a study to first establish feasibility of the study and then to register the treatment data of adult patients with a diagnosis of intermediate risk of prostate cancer presenting for definitive radiation treatment with either proton radiotherapy or Intensity Modulated Radiation Therapy (IMRT). The investigators propose to employ a hypofractionated strategy with our image guided treatment to further improve cancer control and decrease toxicity.
related_trials.eligibility_brief:
Inclusion Criteria

* Histologically confirmed prostate adenocarcinoma within 365 days of registration.
* Clinical stages T1a-T2c N0 M0 (AJCC Criteria 6th Ed). For any suspicious pelvic lymph node \> 1.5cm (as exhibited on pelvic imaging), biopsy of the lymph node is suggested.
* Histological…
related_trials.answers_question: Long-term biochemical and metastasis-free control with protons vs IMRT
related_trials.relevance_phrase: proton vs IMRT hypofx, improve cancer control

studies.name: PROTEUS
studies.tldr: Most distant mets PSMA PET-detected (53.0% APA vs 60.7% control); preview NEJM data, MFS benefit's clinical meaning contested.
details: 📊 Preview read: results just posted (NEJM online), full ASCO26 presentation pending; framed as highly controversial.
📊 Most distant mets identified by PSMA PET, not conventional imaging: 53.0% apalutamide vs 60.7% control.
⚠️ MFS read confounded: majority of MFS events were PET-detected, raising lead-time / detection-bias concern.
🔍 Design: apalutamide + ADT vs ADT alone (two randomized arms); endpoints include MFS/EFS and BCR.
⚠️ Companion thought experiment (explicitly hypothetical, not PROTEUS data): rescaled 50 vs 60 BCR/100 suggests PET-detection differences could drive much of the metastasis delta.
⚠️ 'Homerun vs largest negative' per curator; primary effect size not reported in source, await full data.

verdict.rationale: Preview NEJM data; primary effect size not in source. Most MFS events PSMA PET-detected (53.0% vs 60.7%), raising lead-time/detection bias that undermines the MFS read.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Randomized, **apalutamide + ADT vs ADT alone**. Results just posted (NEJM online); full ASCO26 presentation pending, so this is a preview read.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: **Most distant metastases were PSMA PET-detected**: 53.0% in the apalutamide group vs 60.7% in the control group. Primary MFS effect size not reported in source.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: MFS events were predominantly PET-detected, raising **lead-time / detection-bias** concerns about the endpoint. The 50-vs-60 BCR/100 figures circulating are an **illustrative hypothetical**, not PROTEUS primary data.

analysis_sections.label: Discussion
analysis_sections.body: Curator frames it as **'homerun vs largest negative'** pending full data. A companion thought experiment argues PSMA PET vs conventional detection differences could account for much of the apparent metastasis delta.

open_questions: Does the MFS benefit hold on OS or conventional-imaging endpoints?
How much of the MFS delta reflects PSMA PET lead-time bias?

related_trials.brief_title: An Efficacy and Safety Study of JNJ-56021927 (Apalutamide) in High-risk Prostate Cancer Subjects Receiving Primary Radiation Therapy: ATLAS
related_trials.brief_summary: The purpose of this study is to determine if apalutamide plus gonadotropin releasing hormone (GnRH) agonist in participants with high-risk, localized or locally advanced prostate cancer receiving primary radiation therapy (RT) results in an improvement of metastasis-free survival (MFS) based on conventional imaging assessed by blinded independent central review (BICR).
related_trials.eligibility_brief:
Inclusion Criteria:

* Age \>= 18 years
* Indicated and planned to receive primary radiation therapy for prostate cancer
* Histologically confirmed adenocarcinoma of an intact prostate, and 1 of the following at diagnosis: 1) Gleason score \>=8 and \>=cT2c, 2) Gleason score 7, PSA \>=20 nanogram…
related_trials.answers_question: Does the MFS benefit hold on OS or conventional-imaging endpoints?

studies.name: CAN-2409
studies.tldr: DFS HR 0.70 (0.52-0.94, p=0.016) adding intraprostatic CAN-2409 to EBRT in intermediate/high-risk localised prostate; OS and PCSM immature.
details: 🔍 Phase 3 double-blind placebo-controlled RCT, 2:1 randomisation, N=745, 51 US/Puerto Rico centres
🔍 Primary EP disease-free survival (ITT); accrued 2012-2021, median follow-up 50.3mo
🔍 RT: EBRT 78Gy/2Gy or hypofx (60Gy/3Gy, 70Gy/2.5Gy); ADT optional
💊 3 intraprostatic aglatimagene injections (5×10^11 viral particles) + valacyclovir vs placebo + valacyclovir
⚠️ DFS is a composite (recurrence or death); OS and PCSM immature, 1 PCSM event per arm
⚠️ ADT optional, no brachy/SIB boost mandated; added value over modern dose intensification unresolved
⚠️ Accrual 2012-2021 predates PSMA staging; 'high-risk' cohort likely differs from PSMA-staged pts today

details.text: 📊 Grade 3+ safety comparable; no treatment-related deaths

verdict.rationale: Positive phase 3 primary (DFS) but on a surrogate/composite; OS and PCSM immature (1 event/arm). Additive benefit over modern dose-intensification plus ADT unproven.
verdict.audience: Intermediate or high-risk localised prostate cancer, EBRT candidates

studies.significance: The RT read is local control: 2yr biopsy positivity fell 36.4% to 19.6% (p=0.0015), comparable to adding ADT (RTOG 9408) but on standard 78Gy without a brachy or SIB boost. Whether it adds over modern dose-intensification plus ADT is the open integration question, since ADT was optional and no boost was used.
studies.monday_clinic: In intermediate or high-risk localised prostate cancer planned for definitive EBRT, this informs whether an added intraprostatic biologic could improve local control; it does not extend to post-prostatectomy, salvage, or metastatic settings.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: **Phase 3**, double-blind, placebo-controlled RCT, **2:1** randomisation, **N=745** across 51 US and Puerto Rico centres. Median follow-up **50.3 mo**.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: **Intermediate or high-risk localised** prostate cancer, ECOG 0-2, planned for EBRT. Stratified by risk category and ADT use. 79% White, 16% Black.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: EBRT **78 Gy/2 Gy**, or hypofractionated **60 Gy/3 Gy** or **70 Gy/2.5 Gy**. ADT optional. **No dose-escalation or brachytherapy boost** mandated.

analysis_sections.label: Regimen
analysis_sections.body: Three intraprostatic aglatimagene injections (**5×10^11 viral particles**) plus valacyclovir prodrug, versus placebo plus valacyclovir.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **Primary:** disease-free survival (recurrence or death, ITT). Secondary/exploratory: OS, PCSM, 2-year post-RT biopsy pathologic response.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: **Primary met:** DFS favoured aglatimagene; **OS and PCSM immature**, not significantly different. Arm-by-endpoint values in the results figure.

analysis_sections.label: Safety
analysis_sections.body: Grade 3+ TEAEs **8% vs 7%**; most common acute kidney injury (2% both arms). Serious AEs 6% vs 7%. **No treatment-related deaths**.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: Discussant benchmarked the biopsy-positivity gain against **ADT (RTOG 9408)**, **brachytherapy boost (ASCENDE-RT)**, and **SIB (FLAME)** as established local-control intensifiers.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents intermediate or high-risk localised prostate cancer treated with definitive EBRT. Does not represent post-prostatectomy, salvage, or metastatic disease.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: DFS is a **composite surrogate**; OS and PCSM immature. **ADT optional, no boost mandated**, confounding added value over modern intensification. Accrual (2012-2021) predates PSMA staging.

open_questions: Does the local-control gain add over modern dose-escalation and ADT?
Do DFS and pCR gains translate to overall or cancer-specific survival?
Optimal ADT and RT-intensification pairing with intraprostatic CAN-2409

sites.intro: First targeted 2L option to roughly double OS over chemo in metastatic pancreatic; no RT relevance.

studies.name: RASolute 302
studies.tldr: mOS 13.2 vs 6.7mo, HR 0.40 (0.30-0.53), P<0.001 for daraxonrasib vs investigator's-choice chemo in previously treated metastatic pancreatic cancer.
details: 🔍 Phase 3 RCT: daraxonrasib (oral RAS(ON) multi-selective inhibitor) vs investigator's-choice chemo in previously treated metastatic pancreatic cancer
🔍 Two prespecified populations: RAS G12-mutant (n=228 vs 231) and overall (n=248 vs 252; includes G12/G13/Q61 + no RAS mutation identified)
📊 Death events 32% (daraxonrasib) vs 55-56% (chemo), both populations; medians reached
💊 Single oral targeted agent standing in for cytotoxic chemo at 2L; targets active GTP-bound RAS across G12 variants
🔗 Chemo control (6.6-6.7mo) matches the historic 2L metastatic PDAC benchmark; experimental arm 13.2mo roughly doubles it
⚠️ Data from a single ASCO LBA OS figure; full efficacy, safety, and PFS not yet published (abstract 'soon')
⚠️ Open-label (oral drug vs IV chemo); OS is a hard endpoint so bias is limited, but any PRO/PFS read needs blinded review
⚠️ Daraxonrasib median OS upper CI not reached (10.0-NR); tail durability still immature

verdict.rationale: Randomized phase 3, OS primary hit HR 0.40 in 2L metastatic PDAC where no targeted SOC exists; hard endpoint, unprecedented magnitude. Full LBA data pending.
verdict.audience: Previously treated metastatic pancreatic cancer (RAS-mut or WT)

studies.monday_clinic: In previously treated metastatic pancreatic cancer with a RAS mutation, daraxonrasib's OS benefit supports it as a systemic option over chemotherapy; the signal does not extend to treatment-naive or localized disease.

primary_endpoint.name: Overall survival
primary_endpoint.stat_detail: 95% CI 0.30-0.53, P<0.001; mOS 13.2 vs 6.7 mo (overall pop)

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: **Phase 3 RCT**, daraxonrasib vs investigator's-choice chemotherapy in previously treated metastatic pancreatic cancer. Two prespecified populations: **RAS G12-mutant** and **overall** (G12/G13/Q61 or no RAS mutation identified).

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Previously treated metastatic pancreatic cancer. RAS G12 arm **n=228 vs 231**; overall **n=248 vs 252**. Overall population also includes tumors with no identified RAS mutation.

analysis_sections.label: Regimen
analysis_sections.body: **Daraxonrasib**, an oral RAS(ON) multi-selective inhibitor targeting active GTP-bound RAS, vs investigator's choice cytotoxic chemotherapy.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: **Primary OS met**: median **13.2 vs 6.7 mo**, **HR 0.40** (0.30-0.53), P<0.001 (overall); HR 0.40 (0.30-0.54) in RAS G12. 12-mo OS **53% vs 17%** (overall). Deaths 32% vs 55-56%.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents previously treated metastatic pancreatic cancer with RAS mutations (G12/G13/Q61) or no identified RAS mutation. Does not represent treatment-naive or localized/resectable pancreatic cancer.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: Chemo control (6.6-6.7 mo) matches the historic 2L metastatic PDAC benchmark; no prior targeted agent has shown an OS signal of this magnitude in this setting.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Data limited to a single ASCO LBA OS figure; full safety and PFS not yet reported. **Open-label**; daraxonrasib median OS upper CI not reached (10.0-NR), so tail durability is immature.

open_questions: Efficacy across RAS-WT vs RAS-mutant subsets

related_trials.brief_title: Study of Daraxonrasib and Daraxonrasib + GnP as First-line Treatment in Patients With Metastatic Pancreatic Adenocarcinoma
related_trials.brief_summary: The purpose of this study is to evaluate the safety and efficacy of an investigational RAS(ON) inhibitor administered as monotherapy or in combination with chemotherapy, compared with standard of care (SOC) chemotherapy alone.
related_trials.eligibility_brief:
Inclusion Criteria:

* At least 18 years old and has provided informed consent.
* Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group (ECOG) performance status of 0 or 1.
* Histologically or cytologically confirmed pancreatic adenocarcinoma.
* Diagnosis of metastatic disease ≤ 6 weeks prior to informed consent.
*…

sites.intro: RT delivered first as an immune primer in MSS metastatic rectal; provocative conversion signal but single-arm, responses not yet durable.

studies.name: MIRACLE-2
studies.tldr: 68% ORR, mOS 23.2mo, 18% NED with RT-primed chemo + tislelizumab in MSS unresectable met rectal ca.
details: 🔍 Prospective single-arm phase I, N=50, Fudan Shanghai; RT delivered FIRST as an immune primer
⚠️ RT dose/fractionation and target volumes not reported in source (HFRT to primary, HFRT/SBRT to mets)
💊 Post-RT, biomarker-gated: FOLFOX-bev-tislelizumab (RAS/BRAF-mut) or FOLFIRI-cetuximab-tisle (WT); tisle 200mg Q2W
📊 18% (9/50) reached NED via conversion resection/ablation or watch-and-wait

details.text: ⚠️ Heme toxicity dominant (RT + FOLFOX/FOLFIRI + PD1); no grade 5 TRAEs

details: ⚠️ Primary EP is ETS (≥20% shrinkage at 8wk), a surrogate; single-arm can't isolate RT's contribution
⚠️ High ORR (68%) but short DOR and low 1-yr DOR rate → responses not yet durable

verdict.rationale: Single-arm phase I, N=50; surrogate primary (ETS at 8wk); no comparator to isolate RT's contribution to the immune-priming effect.
verdict.audience: 1L unresectable MSS rectal cancer, synchronous liver/lung mets

studies.significance: The RT-relevant read is technique: HFRT to the primary plus HFRT/SBRT to mets, delivered first to prime immunity, but no dose or fractionation in source, so it doesn't transfer to practice yet. 18% (9/50) converted to NED via resection or watch-and-wait. Near-universal lymphopenia (95.9% all-grade, 36.7% G3/4) undercuts a strategy premised on RT-driven T-cell activation.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Prospective single-arm phase I, N=50, Fudan University Shanghai Cancer Center. Data cutoff Dec 31 2025; median follow-up 19.9 mo (95% CI 16.4-23.4).

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: MSS rectal cancer, primary ≤10cm from anal verge, synchronous unresectable mets. 76% male, median age 57; 52% liver, 8% lung, 40% both; RAS/BRAF-mut 56%.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: **RT delivered first** as an immune primer: HFRT to the primary, HFRT or SBRT to metastases. **Dose, fractionation, and target volumes not reported in source.**

analysis_sections.label: Regimen
analysis_sections.body: Post-RT, biomarker-gated: **FOLFOX-bevacizumab-tislelizumab** (RAS/BRAF-mut) or **FOLFIRI-cetuximab-tislelizumab** (WT); tislelizumab 200mg Q2W. Resection/metastasectomy if converted, watch-and-wait if primary cCR.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **Primary: ETS rate** (≥20% target shrinkage at 8wk). Secondary: DCR, DOR, OS, PFS, safety.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: ETS **76.0%**, ORR **68.0%**, DCR 88.0%; **18% (9/50) reached NED**. Median OS 23.2mo, PFS 9.3mo, DOR 8.0mo (endpoints in table).

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents treatment-naive MSS rectal cancer with synchronous unresectable liver/lung metastases. Does not represent MSI-H tumors, resectable disease, or non-rectal colorectal primaries.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Single-arm phase I, N=50, no comparator to isolate RT's contribution. Surrogate primary (ETS at 8wk), short median DOR (8mo), and near-universal lymphopenia (95.9%).

open_questions: RT's added benefit over chemo plus PD1 alone in MSS mCRC
Optimal RT dose and fractionation for immune priming in MSS mCRC
Predictive biomarker for MSS response to trimodal therapy

related_trials.brief_title: Regorafenib Alone or in Combination With Hypofractionated/Low-dose Radiotherapy Plus Toripalimab for Metastatic Colorectal Cancer

Patients are randomly assigned (1:1) into the control arm and the experimental arm. Control arm: a total of 54 patients will receive regorafenib monotherapy. Experimental arm: a total of 54 patients will first receive 1 cycle of regorafenib and…

2. An Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group (ECOG) performance status ≤1
3. Life expectancy of at least 3 months
4. Histopathological confirmed MSS/pMMR adenocarcinoma of the colon or rectum
5. At least one evaluable metastatic lesion for radiotherapy…

related_trials.brief_title: Standard Systemic Therapy Combined With High/Low-dose Radiotherapy Plus Toripalimab for Metastatic Colorectal Cancer
related_trials.brief_summary: TORCH-M is a prospective, single-arm, two-cohort, investigator-initiated phase II trial to investigate the efficacy and safety of standard systemic therapy in combination with high/low-dose radiotherapy plus toripalimab in paitents with microsatellite stable metastatic colorectal cancer (MSS mCRC). Eligible patients will be assigned to two cohorts according to previous treatment: a first-line cohort A and a second-line cohort B. Patients in both arms will first receive one cycle of standard…

interventions.name: standard systemic therapy combined with high/low-dose radiotherapy plus toripalimab

An Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group (ECOG) performance status ≤1.

At least one evaluable metastatic lesion for radiotherapy and evaluation according to RECIST…
related_trials.answers_question: Optimal RT dose and fractionation for immune priming in MSS mCRC
related_trials.relevance_phrase: high + low-dose RT + toripalimab in MSS mCRC

sites.intro: Real-world only: sequencing RT before ICI beat concurrent iRT for OS, but hypothesis-generating with no RT technique reported.

studies.name: OCEANUS
studies.tldr: Sequential iRT beat concurrent for real-world OS in newly-dx advanced NSCLC: median 20.3 vs 16.0 mo, HR 0.68 (0.47-0.99), P=.045.

details.text: 📊 Real-world OS by iRT comparison (propensity-weighted, landmark)

rows: Newly-dx advanced, sequential vs concurrent iRT
Refractory, RT + ICI maintenance vs RT alone

details: 🔍 Territory-wide real-world cohort (Hong Kong CDARS, >90% population coverage); NSCLC dx 2010-2021 who received iRT
🔍 335 of 3522 ICI-treated pts got RT: 155 newly-dx advanced, 180 refractory. Median age 64 (34-90), 73.7% male
🔍 Overlap-weighting propensity score primary; IPTW sensitivity gave similar estimates
💊 Chemotherapy associated with longer OS in newly-dx; not significant in refractory
⚠️ Observational: sequential vs concurrent not randomized, confounding by indication plausible despite weighting
⚠️ No RT dose, fractionation, modality, or target volume reported in source; authors call findings hypothesis-generating
🔗 Direction (RT before ICI favored) aligns with PACIFIC paradigm, though PACIFIC was curative-intent stage III chemoRT, not advanced disease

verdict.rationale: Observational real-world cohort; sequential-vs-concurrent not randomized, confounding by indication despite propensity weighting. Small subgroups (155 newly-dx). Authors label it hypothesis-generating.
verdict.audience: Advanced/refractory NSCLC on immunoradiotherapy, median age 64, 74% male

studies.significance: Sequential iRT (ICI and RT not overlapping) beat concurrent for real-world OS in newly-dx advanced NSCLC, HR 0.68 (0.47-0.99), P=.045, favoring temporal separation of RT from ICI. RT dose, fractionation, and target volume aren't in the source, so the signal can't yet transfer to a specific plan. The refractory RT+ICI-maintenance read was NS (P=.20).
studies.monday_clinic: In newly-diagnosed advanced NSCLC starting immunoradiotherapy, this real-world signal supports separating RT from ICI in time rather than delivering them concurrently; it does not extend to refractory disease, where the RT-plus-ICI-maintenance benefit was not significant.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Territory-wide **real-world cohort** (OCEANUS, Hong Kong CDARS, >90% population coverage); NSCLC diagnosed 2010-2021 who received iRT. **Overlap-weighting** propensity score primary, IPTW sensitivity; landmark-based OS with weighted Kaplan-Meier and Cox (restricted mean survival time where PH violated).

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: 335 of 3522 ICI-treated pts received RT: **155 newly-dx advanced**, **180 refractory**. Median age **64** (34-90), **73.7% male**. Refractory analysis required survival ≥90 days (landmark).

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: RT dose, fractionation, modality, and target volume **not reported in source**. The variable studied is **timing of RT relative to ICI** (sequential vs concurrent), not technique.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: Both comparisons favored the sequential / ICI-maintenance arm (magnitudes in table). **Chemotherapy** was associated with longer OS in newly-dx pts only; **not significant** in refractory disease.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: Randomized data on iRT sequencing in advanced NSCLC are limited (stated by authors). RT-before-ICI direction is consistent with the **PACIFIC** consolidation paradigm, but PACIFIC treated curative-intent unresectable stage III with concurrent chemoRT, a different population.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents advanced or refractory NSCLC receiving immunoradiotherapy, predominantly male, median age 64. Does not represent curative-intent stage III chemoRT, oligometastatic, or ICI-treated patients who did not receive RT.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Observational: sequential vs concurrent not randomized, residual confounding by indication despite weighting. Small subgroups (155 newly-dx). Refractory comparison **non-significant** (P=.20).

open_questions: Optimal RT-ICI sequencing (sequential vs concurrent) in advanced NSCLC
Value of ICI maintenance after RT in refractory NSCLC

related_trials.brief_title: PD-1 Inhibitor and Chemotherapy With Concurrent Irradiation at Varied Tumour Sites in Advanced Non-small Cell Lung Cancer
related_trials.brief_summary: Overall survival (OS) of patients with advanced (stage IIIB/IV) non-small-cell lung cancer (NSCLC) remains short after the first line of treatment with a median OS of 12.2 months in non squamous NSCLC and 9.2 months in squamous NSCLC . In this setting the programmed death 1/ligand 1 (PD-1/-L1) were targeted with nivolumab (IgG4) in advanced squamous and nonsquamous NSCLC leading to an increase of the 1-year OS rate of approximately 10-15% in both histologies. Nivolumab, pembrolizumab and…

MIRACLE-2: RT to primary/mets -> chemo + tislelizumab in MSS unresectable met rectal ca (N=50): 68% ORR & median OS 23 mo.

Early, single-arm data, but ~1 in 5 pts reached NED.

Suggests RT + systemic + PD1 blockade could overcome immune resistance in MSS mCRC. #ASCO26 @OncoAlert pic.twitter.com/sjnUW8x7f3

Suggests RT + systemic + PD1 blockade could overcome immune resistance in MSS mCRC. #ASCO26 @OncoAlert pic.twitter.com/sjnUW8x7f3

image_ocr_texts:
Abstract 539778: Radiotherapy followed by systemic therapy and Tislelizumab in microsatellite-stable rectal cancer
with synchronous unresectable metastases: Updated results of the MIRACLE-2 study
Menglong Zhou, 'Lijun Shen, 2Zezhi Shan, 'Hui Zhang, Wang Yang, 'Yajie Chen, "Yaqi Wang, 'Shujuan Zhou, 'Ruiyan Wu, Juefeng Wan,
2Dakui Luo, "Zilan Ye, 'Weijing He, "Yufei Yang, 'Yikuan Chen, "Changming Zhou, 'Qingguo Li, "Fan Xia, 'Xinxiang Li, 'Zhen Zhang
Department of Radiation Oncology, -Department of Colorectal Surgery, Department of Cancer Prevention, Fudan University Shanghai Cancer Center, Shanghai, PR China
*Clinical Oncology School of Fujian Medical University, Fujian Cancer Hospital (Fujian Branch of Fudan University Shanghai Cancer Center), Fuzhou, PR China.
Background
Microsatellite-stable (MSS) tumors comprise ~95% of
metastatic colorectal cancer cases and exhibit intrinsic
resistance to immunotherapy. Emerging evidence suggests
systemic therapy may enhance immunotherapy
responsiveness, while radiotherapy could improve efficacy by
overcoming resistance and reducing tumor burden. The
MIRACLE-2 study evaluated the safety and efficacy of
combining radiotherapy with systemic therapy and
Tislelizumab as first-line treatment for unresectable
metastatic MSS rectal cancer (RC).
Methods
MIRACLE-2 was a prospective, single-arm, phase I study.
Inclusion criteria: MSS RC with primary tumor ≤10 cm from
anal verge on MRI and synchronous unresectable
metastases. Patients received hypofractionated radiotherapy
(HFRT) for primary lesions and HFRT or SBRT for metastases,
followed by systemic therapy: FOLFOX-bevacizumab-
Tislelizumab for RAS/BRAF-mutant patients or FOLFIRI-
cetuximab-Tislelizumab for wild-type patients. Reassessment
every 8 weeks. Patients achieving resectable disease
underwent primary tumor resection and
metastasectomy/local ablative therapies for metastases. For
patients unable to preserve anal sphincter, a watch-and-wait
(WW) strategy was considered if clinical complete response
(cCR) of primary tumor was achieved. Otherwise, systemic
therapy continued until progression/intolerable toxicity.
Primary endpoint: ETS rate (≥20% target lesion reduction at 8
weeks post systemic therapy initiation). Secondary endpoints:
disease control rate (DCR), duration of response (DOR), overall
survival (OS), progression-free survival (PFS), and safety.
Results
As of December 31, 2025, efficacy data were available for 50 patients (38
males, 76.0%; median age 57 years, range 30-73). Among these, 52.0%
had liver metastases, 8.0% lung metastases, and 40.0% both liver and
lung metastases. RAS/BRAF mutations were detected in 56.0% of
primary tumors. Median treatment courses: eight (range 3-12). Overall,
18% (9/50) attained no evidence of disease (NED). ETS rate: 76.0%;
DCR: 88.0%. Median follow-up: 19.9 months (95% Cl: 16.4-23.4). Among
34 patients with complete/partial response, median DOR: 8.0 months
(95% Cl: 5.2-10.8), with 1-year DOR rate of 20%. Median PFS: 9.3 months
(95% Cl: 7.1-11.5), with 1-year PFS rate of 33.4%. Median OS: 23.2
months (95% Cl: 15.1-31.3), and 1-year OS rate: 93.3%. No grade 5
treatment-related adverse events (TRAEs) occurred. All-grade TRAEs:
lymphopenia (95.9%), anemia (91.8%), and leukopenia (69.4%). Grade
3/4 TRAEs: lymphopenia (36.7%), neutropenia (26.5%), and leukopenia
(20.4%).
Conclusions
The combination of radiotherapy, systemic therapy, and Tislelizumab
showed a high ETS rate and manageable safety profile in first-line
unresectable MSS RC. Long-term outcomes require further follow-up.
Figure 1. The flowchart of Miracle-2 study

Future Directions for Research
Biomarker Discovery: There is a critical need to identify
predictive biomarkers that can select patients who are most
likely to respond to this trimodal treatment approach, moving
away from a "one-size-fits-all" strategy for MSS rectal cancer.
Acknowledgements
• We thank the FUSCC's Colorectal Cancer MDT for their
support. We also thank the study subjects for their
cooperation.
• Corresponding author: Prof. Zhen ZHANG
E-mail: zhen_zhang@fudan.edu.cn
• Presenting author: Prof. Fan XIA
E-mail: tcxiafan@hotmail.com

Key Takeaway Points
Oncolytic-viral therapy in localized prostate cancer
Intra-prostatic CAN-2409 reduces relative clinical recurrence/death by
30%, driven by higher pathologic CR rates.
Understand role in context of ADT & modern RT dose intensification strategies
Digital-path based Al biomarker to personalize systemic therapy
Supports guiding abiraterone use in non-metastatic very high-risk
prostate cancer patients.
Role of ARPI in modern very high-risk unclear, await further prospective
validation

papers.title: Combination of Radiotherapy and Immunotherapy in Advanced Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer.
papers.abstract:
IMPORTANCE: The optimal sequencing of radiotherapy (RT) combined with immunotherapy (iRT) and the value of chemotherapy remain undefined for advanced non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), where randomized data are limited.

OBJECTIVE: To compare real-world overall survival (OS) between sequential and concurrent iRT in newly diagnosed advanced NSCLC, assess the effect of immune checkpoint inhibitor (ICI) maintenance after RT in refractory disease, and evaluate the association of chemotherapy with survival.

DESIGN, SETTING, AND PARTICIPANTS: This is a territory-wide study (OCEANUS) based on the Hong Kong Hospital Authority Clinical Data Analysis and Reporting System (more than 90% population coverage). Patients with NSCLC diagnosed from January 1, 2010, to December 31, 2021, who subsequently received iRT for advanced or refractory disease were included. Overlap weighting was the primary propensity score-weighted method, with inverse probability of treatment weighting used for sensitivity analysis. Data were analyzed from December 2024 to April 2025.

EXPOSURES: Sequential vs concurrent iRT for newly diagnosed advanced NSCLC; RT with vs without ICI maintenance for refractory NSCLC; receipt of chemotherapy.

MAIN OUTCOMES AND MEASURES: The primary outcome was real-world OS after landmark, estimated with weighted Kaplan-Meier and Cox models. When proportional hazards were violated (per Schoenfeld residuals), treatment effects were summarized using restricted mean survival time.

RESULTS: Of 3522 patients who received ICIs, 335 received RT, including 155 with newly diagnosed advanced and 180 with refractory NSCLC. Of these, 247 (73.7%) were male, and the median (range) age was 64 (34-90) years. In newly diagnosed NSCLC, patients treated with sequential iRT had significant longer real-world OS than those treated with concurrent iRT (median, 20.3 months [95% CI, 13.3 to not reached] vs 16.0 months [95% CI, 8.3-30.0]; adjusted hazard ratio, 0.68; 95% CI, 0.47-0.99; P = .045). Chemotherapy was also associated with longer survival in patients with newly diagnosed advanced NSCLC. In refractory NSCLC, RT with ICI maintenance was associated with a numerically longer median real-world OS (11.2 months [95% CI, 7.9-20.6] vs 6.7 months [95% CI, 4.4-17.4]; P = .20). Addition of chemotherapy was not significant for real-world OS. Inverse probability of treatment weighting analyses produced similar estimates.

CONCLUSIONS AND RELEVANCE: In this cohort study, sequential iRT was associated with longer survival than concurrent iRT in patients with newly diagnosed advanced NSCLC, and chemotherapy was associated with longer survival. In patients with refractory NSCLC who survived at least 90 days, RT with ICI maintenance resulted in nonsignificantly longer survival and an unclear association with chemotherapy. These findings are hypothesis generating and support prospective randomized studies to define optimal sequencing of iRT and use of systemic treatment partners.

papers.title: Aglatimagene besadenovec (CAN-2409) with radiotherapy for patients with localised prostate cancer: a phase 3, multicentre, randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial
papers.abstract: Background About 30% of men with localised prostate cancer undergoing radiotherapy with curative intent have disease recurrence associated with progression-related symptoms and substantial toxicity of salvage therapies. Previous studies with aglatimagene besadenovec (CAN-2409, hereafter referred to as aglatimagene) showed synergy with radiation and immune-mediated cytotoxicity in patients with prostate cancer. We aimed to assess whether addition of aglatimagene plus prodrug (valacyclovir) to standard-of-care external beam radiation therapy (EBRT) could improve disease-free survival in this population. Methods We conducted a phase 3, randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial at 51 medical centres (26 community and 25 institutional or military) across the USA and Puerto Rico in patients with intermediate or high-risk prostate cancer. Patients aged at least 18 years who were planning to undergo EBRT and with an Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group score of 0-2 were eligible. Patients were randomly assigned (2:1) via central block-randomisation to receive either three courses of intraprostatic aglatimagene (5 × 10 11 viral particles) plus valacyclovir or placebo plus valacyclovir, with randomisation stratified by risk category and androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) use. Patients received standard-of-care EBRT (78 Gy in 2 Gy fractions) or hypofractionated EBRT (60 Gy in 3 Gy fractions or 70 Gy in 2·5 Gy fractions) with optional ADT. The primary endpoint was disease-free survival, defined as time-from-randomisation to prostate cancer recurrence or death in the intent-to-treat population (all randomly assigned patients). Safety was assessed in all individuals who received at least one injection. The trial is registered at ClinicalTrials.gov, NCT01436968, and long-term follow-up is ongoing. Findings Between Feb 21, 2012, and Sept 9, 2021, 745 men (591 [79%] White, 121 [16%] Black) were randomly assigned to receive aglatimagene plus valacyclovir (n=496) or placebo plus valacyclovir (n=249). After a median follow-up of 50·3 months (IQR 35·2-63·3), median disease-free survival was not reached (95% CI 121·78 to not reached) in the aglatimagene plus valacyclovir group versus 86·1 (IQR 29·7-143·0) months in the placebo plus valacyclovir group (hazard ratio 0·70, 95% CI 0·52-0·94; p=0·016). Treatment-emergent adverse events (TEAEs) of grade 3 or worse occurred in 40 (8%) of 479 patients in the aglatimagene group and 17 (7%)of 232 patients in the placebo group. The most common TEAE of grade 3 or worse was acute kidney injury in both the aglatimagene group (nine [2%] of 479 patients) and the placebo group (four [2%] of 232 patients). Serious adverse events occurred in 28 (6%) of 479 patients in the aglatimagene group and 17 (7%) of 232 in the placebo group. Treatment-related serious adverse events occurred in eight (2%) patients in the aglatimagene group (four acute kidney injury, two pyrexia, and one each influenza-like symptoms and urinary retention) and five (2%) in the placebo group (four acute kidney injury, and one each increased creatinine levels and skin rash; one patient reported two serious adverse events). No treatment-related deaths were reported. Interpretation Aglatimagene plus valacyclovir was associated with longer disease-free survival than placebo plus valacyclovir when added to standard of radiotherapy for the treatment of localised prostate cancer, offering a meaningful benefit without increasing clinically significant toxicity. Funding Candel Therapeutics and US National Institutes of Health.
